insights: Low-cost expense reports take 4.5x longer to process than high-cost ones (median 32 vs 7 days), revealing a severe structural inefficiency and fairness gap in the processing system.

Expense amount exhibits an extraordinarily strong, universal negative correlation with processing time (Pearson r ≈ -0.995), meaning higher amounts consistently predict dramatically faster resolution across the entire dataset.

This inverse relationship is perfectly monotonic across amount quartiles (Q1→Q4) and holds without exception across all 7 departments — including small ones — indicating the pattern is systemic, not department-specific.
Department explains no additional variance in processing time beyond amount, confirming that cost level — not organizational unit — is the dominant, near-exclusive driver of processing speed.

The right-skewed processing time distribution (10th percentile = 7 days; 75th percentile = 30 days) combined with the extreme cost-based disparity suggests low-cost expenses are disproportionately subject to delays, backlogs, or manual review bottlenecks.

Customer Support and Sales together account for 79% of all records, yet the cost–speed relationship remains equally strong in these large departments and all smaller ones — ruling out data imbalance or outlier concentration as explanations.
summary: The core business goal — understanding how expense cost influences processing time — is decisively answered: cost is the overwhelming, universal determinant of speed, with a near-perfect inverse relationship (r ≈ −0.995) that holds identically across all departments and amount brackets. Low-cost expenses suffer severe processing penalties, taking 32 days median versus just 7 days for high-cost ones — a 4.5x delay that undermines both operational efficiency and procedural fairness. This pattern is not driven by departmental differences, data quality issues, or sample bias, as it persists robustly even in small departments and aligns perfectly with the overall distribution’s right skew. The finding points unambiguously to a systemic policy or workflow issue — likely prioritization rules, automated routing logic, or review thresholds — that unintentionally deprioritizes low-value expenses. Next focus should be auditing approval workflows and triage criteria to identify where and why low-cost reports are delayed.
